_meta.authoringNotes: Cross-checked against AAO BCSC Cornea Section 8, Hirst 2008 pterygium surgery review (PMID 18929129), and PubMed-indexed case series on conjunctival amyloid. ICD-10 H11.04 cited per WHO 2024 listing; systemic amyloid coded under E85. Surgical recurrence figures from Hirst 2008 and Clearfield 2017 Cochrane review.

h1Title: Amyloid Pterygium: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Amyloid pterygium is a rare variant of pterygium in which fibrillar amyloid protein deposits accumulate within the stroma of the conjunctival-corneal growth, giving the lesion a distinctive waxy, yellowish appearance and fragile blood vessels prone to spontaneous bleeding. It accounts for under 1% of all pterygium specimens sent to pathology in temperate climates, but reaches several percent in tropical, high-ultraviolet regions of South Asia, Africa, and Oceania. Patients present with the same foreign body irritation, redness, and astigmatism as standard pterygium, yet the waxy texture and bleeding tendency should prompt biopsy. Congo red staining with apple-green birefringence under polarized light confirms amyloid, and a small subset of cases signal underlying systemic amyloidosis that requires hematology workup. Surgical excision with conjunctival autograft remains the standard of care.

keyStats.prevalence: Under 1% of pterygium specimens in temperate climates; 1-4% in tropical high-UV regions per histopathology series
keyStats.demographics: Adults aged 40-70, outdoor workers, men slightly more affected than women
keyStats.avgAge: Most patients are diagnosed between 45 and 65 years
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global counts unknown; pterygium itself affects 10-12% of adults in equatorial belts (WHO Vision 2020)

definition: Amyloid pterygium (ICD-10: H11.04 for pterygium, peripheral, stationary; with reference to systemic amyloidosis E85 when relevant) is a histologically defined subtype of pterygium in which amorphous, eosinophilic, congophilic amyloid fibrils deposit within the substantia propria of the conjunctival growth that encroaches onto the cornea. Standard pterygium is a fibrovascular wing of degenerated conjunctival tissue driven by chronic ultraviolet exposure, dust, and dry climate. In amyloid pterygium, the same fibrovascular base coexists with abnormal protein deposits that stain salmon-pink with Congo red and exhibit apple-green dichroism under crossed polarizers — the histochemical signature of amyloid. The amyloid may be a primary localized conjunctival amyloidosis confined to the lesion, or it may reflect systemic light-chain (AL), transthyretin (ATTR), or reactive (AA) amyloidosis that has chosen the conjunctiva as an early biopsy-accessible site. Clinically, the lesion looks waxy, yellow-orange, and bulkier than a standard pterygium, and the abnormal vessels embedded in amyloid stroma bleed easily with minor trauma. The condition is managed by cornea-trained ophthalmologists, with input from hematology when systemic amyloid is identified.

typesClassification.description: Amyloid deposition confined to the pterygium tissue with no evidence of systemic disease. Most cases fall in this group and behave like an unusual pterygium with no extra-ocular implications.

Amyloid in the pterygium represents the deposition of a systemic protein — most often AL (immunoglobulin light chain) or ATTR (transthyretin). Immunohistochemistry on the excised tissue, plus serum and urine workup, identify the systemic disease.
Regrowth after surgery, more likely if a bare sclera technique was used or if adjacent conjunctiva harbours residual amyloid. Recurrences may be more vascular and bulkier than the original lesion.

typesClassification.type: Amyloid pterygium with co-existing primary acquired melanosis or carcinoma in situ
typesClassification.description: Rare overlap reported in case series; pigmented or atypical-looking lesions warrant excisional biopsy with margin marking to exclude ocular surface squamous neoplasia.

primarySymptoms: A waxy, yellow-pink wedge of tissue growing from the nasal or temporal conjunctiva onto the cornea, with a thicker, more gelatinous look than a standard pterygium.
Foreign body sensation and intermittent grittiness that worsens with wind, dust, sun, and time spent on screens.
Recurrent redness over the lesion, often persisting even after lubricating drops, due to dilated abnormal vessels in the amyloid stroma.
Episodes of spontaneous or minor-trauma bleeding on the surface of the lesion — a clue that distinguishes amyloid pterygium from the standard form, where bleeding is uncommon.
Blurring of vision when the lesion crosses 2 millimetres onto the cornea or induces irregular astigmatism that destabilizes the tear film.
A visible cosmetic deformity that patients describe as a yellowish growth on the eye, often more noticeable than a typical pterygium because of its waxy color.
Tearing and reflex epiphora from chronic surface irritation and tear film disruption over the apex of the lesion.
Slow progression over months to years, sometimes with sudden bulkier growth episodes that prompt clinical review.
Dryness and a sense of ocular fatigue, especially in patients who spend hours outdoors without ultraviolet-blocking eyewear.
Photophobia in advanced cases where the lesion approaches the visual axis or causes ocular surface inflammation.
earlyWarningSigns: A pterygium that looks more yellow, waxy, or fleshy than expected, or that bleeds with minor rubbing
Astigmatism increasing on annual refraction without other corneal pathology
Episodes of unilateral subconjunctival haemorrhage over an existing pterygium
Family history of amyloidosis (AL, ATTR-CM, hATTR) or unexplained heart failure or peripheral neuropathy in a relative
emergencySigns: Sudden marked visual loss, severe pain, or hypopyon over a pterygium-bearing eye — possible infection or scleritis requiring urgent ophthalmology review
Profuse spontaneous ocular bleeding that fails to settle with simple pressure
New-onset orthostatic light-headedness, ankle oedema, or fainting in a patient diagnosed with amyloid pterygium — possible systemic AL amyloidosis with cardiac involvement
Rapid bilateral progression of conjunctival lesions with proptosis or restricted eye movement — possible orbital amyloid or lymphoma

causes.description: UV-B radiation damages the limbal stem cell barrier, drives elastotic degeneration of conjunctival collagen, and is the most consistent environmental driver of pterygium of any subtype. Cumulative outdoor exposure over decades is the dominant risk.

Within the pterygium tissue, fibrillar proteins fold into beta-pleated amyloid sheets and accumulate around blood vessels. Most localized conjunctival amyloid is AL-type from a benign clonal plasma-cell population in the lesion itself.

A minority of cases — case series suggest 5-15% — are the ocular manifestation of systemic amyloid: monoclonal light-chain (AL) disease, transthyretin (ATTR), or chronic-inflammation-driven AA amyloid. Identifying these changes the prognosis and treatment plan.
Long-standing conjunctivitis, dry eye, blepharitis, or atopic eye disease produces an inflammatory milieu that may favour fibril deposition in a pre-existing pterygium.

causes.cause: Dust, wind, and dry climate exposure
causes.description: Environmental abrasion from agricultural, fishing, and construction work in arid regions accelerates pterygium growth and is documented in epidemiology studies from Australia, India, and sub-Saharan Africa.

riskFactors.description: Lifetime UV dose correlates strongly with pterygium incidence. The risk doubles for workers with more than five hours of daily outdoor sun exposure over 10+ years (Hirst 2008 review of Australian cohorts).

riskFactors.factor: Residence in tropical or equatorial climates
riskFactors.description: Pterygium prevalence in the equatorial belt (latitude under 30°) reaches 10-12% versus under 2% above latitude 40°. Amyloid variants are more frequent in these regions.
Cumulative UV exposure, conjunctival ageing, and declining tear film quality combine to make pterygium most common between 40 and 70 years.
Men account for roughly 55-65% of pterygium cases globally, largely because outdoor occupations such as farming, fishing, and construction remain male-dominated in high-prevalence regions.

riskFactors.factor: Plasma cell dyscrasia or monoclonal gammopathy
riskFactors.description: MGUS, smouldering myeloma, and overt multiple myeloma can seed AL amyloid in any tissue, including the conjunctiva. A monoclonal protein on serum electrophoresis raises suspicion.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis
riskFactors.description: Pathogenic TTR variants such as Val30Met and Val122Ile (carrier rate ~3-4% in Black populations of West African ancestry) predispose to ATTR amyloid that can deposit in soft tissues, including conjunctiva.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic inflammatory disease (rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, untreated infections)
riskFactors.description: Long-standing inflammation drives serum amyloid A elevation and risks reactive AA amyloid deposition. Effective disease control lowers this risk.
Habitual use of sunglasses with UV400 and wide-brimmed hats reduces incident UV at the conjunctiva by 50-90%, lowering pterygium incidence and progression.

riskFactors.factor: Prior pterygium surgery with bare sclera technique
riskFactors.description: Bare sclera excision has 30-90% recurrence rates (Hirst 2008); recurrent tissue may show secondary amyloid deposition and bulkier behaviour than the original lesion.

affectedDemographics: Pterygium prevalence: 10-12% in adults in equatorial regions, under 2% in temperate latitudes (WHO Vision 2020)
Amyloid variant: well-documented case series from India, Saudi Arabia, Nigeria, and northern Australia indicate higher rates than in Europe or North America
Most patients are 45-70 years old at diagnosis; pediatric amyloid pterygium is exceptionally rare and warrants genetic workup
Outdoor workers (farmers, fishermen, construction, military) carry disproportionate disease burden across all geographies
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis starts with a slit-lamp examination. The cornea specialist looks for the wedge-shaped conjunctival growth crossing the limbus onto the cornea, and notes the waxy, fleshy, yellow-pink texture and the fragile vessels that suggest amyloid deposition rather than standard pterygium. Corneal topography quantifies any induced astigmatism, which guides the timing of surgery — visually significant astigmatism developing under 3-4 millimetres of corneal encroachment is unusual and adds to suspicion. Anterior segment optical coherence tomography can show the depth and thickness of the lesion. The diagnosis is confirmed histopathologically after excisional biopsy: Congo red staining produces the salmon-pink colour and the pathognomonic apple-green birefringence under polarized light, and thioflavin T fluorescence supports the finding. Immunohistochemistry — or mass spectrometry where available — identifies the amyloid subtype (AL kappa, AL lambda, ATTR, AA, or others). When AL or ATTR amyloid is found, the patient needs a systemic workup: serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation, serum free light chains, complete blood count, renal and liver function, NT-proBNP and troponin, ECG, and echocardiogram screening for amyloid cardiomyopathy. An abdominal fat-pad fine-needle aspirate is a quick low-risk screen for systemic amyloid. Differentiating amyloid pterygium from ocular surface squamous neoplasia, primary acquired melanosis, conjunctival lymphoma, and standard pterygium with secondary haemorrhage is essential; the histology is decisive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents lesion size, crossing of the limbus onto the cornea, the waxy or yellow appearance, vessel fragility, and any associated dry eye or surface inflammation. Forms the basis for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician seats you at the slit-lamp microscope, places drops to numb the eye if needed, and views the lesion under several magnifications. The exam takes 5-10 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies induced astigmatism and irregularity. Helps decide when to operate — a sudden rise in cylinder over 1.5 dioptres or astigmatism crossing the visual axis is a surgical trigger.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You rest your chin on a topographer for a 30-second non-contact scan. Refraction is the standard eye chart test. Both are painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Excisional biopsy with Congo red and thioflavin T staining
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard confirmation. Salmon-pink Congo red uptake with apple-green birefringence under crossed polarizers is pathognomonic for amyloid. Thioflavin T fluorescence supports the finding.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The lesion is removed under topical or subconjunctival anaesthesia in a 30-45 minute outpatient procedure; the specimen is sent to pathology and results return in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Immunohistochemistry or mass spectrometry for amyloid subtyping
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies whether the amyloid is AL (kappa/lambda), ATTR, AA, or other rare subtypes. Subtype determines whether a systemic workup and disease-specific therapy are needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the same excised specimen by the pathology lab. No additional procedure for the patient; results return alongside the histology report.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation and free light chains
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for a monoclonal protein that would point to AL amyloidosis or plasma-cell dyscrasia. A normal pattern makes systemic AL unlikely.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw plus a 24-hour or spot urine sample. Results return within 3-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: ECG, NT-proBNP, troponin, and transthoracic echocardiogram
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects amyloid cardiomyopathy. Low-voltage ECG with pseudoinfarct pattern and echocardiographic septal thickening with apical sparing on strain imaging are suggestive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ECG and blood tests take under 30 minutes; echocardiogram is a 30-45 minute non-invasive ultrasound. Arranged through cardiology when systemic disease is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A low-risk bedside screen for systemic amyloid; positive in 60-80% of AL cases when stained with Congo red.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fine needle samples fat from the abdomen under local anaesthetic in a 10-minute outpatient procedure. Mild bruising may follow.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of amyloid pterygium is led by surgery, with parallel medical and systemic care depending on the histology. Asymptomatic, small, stable lesions can be observed every 6-12 months with lubricant drops, ultraviolet-blocking sunglasses, and a wide-brimmed hat. Indications for surgery include visually significant astigmatism, encroachment to within 3 millimetres of the visual axis, recurrent inflammation, recurrent bleeding, restricted eye movement from a fibrotic lesion, or cosmetic distress. The standard of care is excision with conjunctival autograft, in which healthy conjunctiva from the superior bulbar surface of the same eye is rotated to cover the bare scleral bed left after lesion removal. Hirst's 2008 review and subsequent Cochrane analyses report recurrence under 5% with autograft compared with 30-90% for the older bare sclera technique, which should no longer be used. Adjunctive mitomycin C 0.02% applied intraoperatively for 1-2 minutes lowers recurrence further in high-risk eyes — recurrent pterygium, large lesions, or fibrovascular activity — at the cost of rare but serious scleral melt if dosing is excessive. Amniotic membrane transplantation is an alternative when the donor conjunctiva is inadequate, with recurrence rates between those of bare sclera and autograft. Postoperatively, patients use topical corticosteroid drops tapered over 6-8 weeks and lubricants for 3-6 months. If histology returns AL amyloid with a positive systemic workup, the patient is referred to a haematology or amyloidosis centre for proteasome inhibitor-based regimens such as bortezomib-cyclophosphamide-dexamethasone or daratumumab combinations. ATTR amyloid is referred for tafamidis (cardiomyopathy) or patisiran/inotersen (polyneuropathy) where indicated. AA amyloid is managed by controlling the underlying inflammatory disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Preservative-free artificial tears and ocular surface lubrication
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for symptom control in observation-phase lesions and during the months after surgery. Reduces foreign body sensation and stabilizes the tear film overlying the pterygium.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 60-75% of mildly symptomatic patients; does not slow growth.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroid drops (fluorometholone 0.1% or dexamethasone 0.1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Used postoperatively in a tapered course over 6-8 weeks to suppress inflammation and reduce recurrence. Intraocular pressure must be monitored, especially in steroid responders.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recurrence reduction of around 30-40% versus no steroid taper; pressure rise in 5-10% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical cyclosporine 0.05% or lifitegrast 5%
medicalTreatments.description: Considered when steroid-related pressure rise is a concern, for chronic surface inflammation, or in eyes with associated dry eye disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in symptom scores in roughly 50-70% of patients over 3-6 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Intraoperative mitomycin C 0.02% (one to two minutes on bare sclera)
medicalTreatments.description: Antifibrotic alkylating agent that lowers recurrence in high-risk eyes after pterygium excision. Used in carefully selected cases; copious irrigation and limited contact time are essential.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recurrence drops to 3-10% with autograft plus mitomycin C versus 5-15% with autograft alone in high-risk lesions; rare scleral melt with overuse.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic anti-plasma-cell or anti-amyloid therapy (haematology-led)
medicalTreatments.description: If histology and workup confirm AL amyloid: bortezomib-cyclophosphamide-dexamethasone or daratumumab regimens. ATTR amyloid: tafamidis for cardiomyopathy, patisiran/inotersen for polyneuropathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AL amyloid haematologic response in 60-80% with modern regimens; tafamidis reduces ATTR cardiomyopathy mortality by 30% (ATTR-ACT trial).

surgicalOptions.name: Pterygium excision with conjunctival autograft
surgicalOptions.description: Current standard of care. The pterygium head and body are dissected from the cornea and sclera, the underlying Tenon's capsule is debulked, and a free conjunctival graft from the superior bulbar surface of the same eye is sutured or fibrin-glued onto the bare sclera.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence under 5% in most series (Hirst 2008); visual and cosmetic improvement in over 90% of selected patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Pterygium excision with amniotic membrane transplant
surgicalOptions.description: An alternative when the donor conjunctiva is scarred, glaucoma surgery is planned, or the defect is too large for autograft alone. Cryopreserved amniotic membrane covers the bare sclera and supports re-epithelialization.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 5-15% in modern series; useful in re-do surgery or combined cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Excision with intraoperative mitomycin C plus autograft
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for recurrent or aggressive lesions. Mitomycin C is applied to the bare sclera before grafting and irrigated copiously to limit toxicity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 3-10% in high-risk eyes; small risk of scleral thinning or melt.

surgicalOptions.name: Lamellar keratoplasty or sclerokeratoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Considered in rare cases where amyloid pterygium has caused deep corneal scarring, perforation risk, or unmanageable astigmatism after standard excision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual recovery in 60-80% of selected eyes; reserved for tertiary cornea centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Strict ultraviolet protection (UV400 wraparound sunglasses and wide-brimmed hats)
alternativeTreatments.description: The single best non-surgical measure for slowing pterygium progression and reducing recurrence risk after surgery. Recommended at all latitudes but especially essential in tropical regions and at altitude.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cool compresses and humidified indoor air
alternativeTreatments.description: Reduce surface inflammation and dry eye in patients with dust- and wind-exposed work environments. Useful adjuncts during the preoperative observation period and postoperative recovery.

whySeeSpecialist: A cornea-trained ophthalmologist should evaluate any pterygium with a waxy or yellow appearance, fragile bleeding vessels, rapid growth, or astigmatism over 1.5 dioptres. The specialist confirms the diagnosis on slit-lamp examination, plans the timing of surgery, and arranges histology with amyloid staining. When systemic amyloid is suspected or confirmed, coordinated care with a haematology or amyloidosis centre is essential — the systemic disease, not the eye lesion, drives long-term prognosis in those cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a fellowship-trained cornea and external disease ophthalmologist with experience in conjunctival autograft surgery and access to pathology that performs Congo red, thioflavin T, and amyloid immunohistochemistry. Ask the surgeon about their personal recurrence rate, their preferred technique (autograft versus amniotic membrane), and how they handle high-risk cases (recurrence, large lesions, amyloid). Continuity of care matters because amyloid pterygium can recur years later and may require repeat assessment.
preventionStrategies: Wear UV400 wraparound sunglasses and a wide-brimmed hat whenever outdoors, especially between 10:00 and 16:00 and at altitude or near reflective surfaces such as water and snow
Use preservative-free lubricating drops several times daily if you work in dusty, windy, or air-conditioned environments to reduce surface stress on the conjunctiva
Treat chronic blepharitis, dry eye, and meibomian gland dysfunction early — sustained surface inflammation favours pterygium growth and amyloid deposition
Get an annual ophthalmology review if you have an established pterygium, so that growth, astigmatism, and any change in appearance suggesting amyloid can be detected early
Address underlying systemic inflammation (rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, chronic infection) to reduce the risk of secondary AA amyloid deposition
lifestyleModifications: Schedule outdoor work in cooler, earlier, or later hours when ultraviolet B intensity is lower, particularly in tropical regions
Use a humidifier in dry indoor environments and rest the eyes every 20 minutes during prolonged screen work to maintain a healthy tear film
Avoid eye rubbing and minor trauma to the lesion — amyloid pterygium vessels bleed easily and rubbing can worsen inflammation
Keep blood pressure and cardiovascular risk factors well controlled, especially if systemic ATTR amyloid is identified
Stay hydrated and avoid prolonged exposure to wind, dust, smoke, and welding without proper protective eyewear

recommended: A Mediterranean-style diet rich in omega-3 fish, leafy greens, and antioxidants that support ocular surface health
Adequate hydration with 1.5-2 litres of water daily unless restricted for cardiac or renal reasons
Foods with vitamin A (eggs, dairy, carrots, sweet potatoes) and lutein and zeaxanthin (kale, spinach) for general ocular health
Sources of vitamin C and E (citrus, berries, nuts, seeds) that contribute to antioxidant defence
Lean protein sources such as fish and legumes to support post-surgical healing
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which worsens dry eye and may interact with postoperative steroid drops
High-salt diets if amyloid cardiomyopathy is identified, where fluid balance is critical
Smoking and second-hand smoke, both of which irritate the ocular surface and impair healing
Long fasts without electrolyte care in patients with systemic amyloid, where blood pressure regulation may be impaired
Highly processed foods with limited antioxidant value during preoperative and postoperative periods

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise is safe and recommended in stable amyloid pterygium. After surgery, avoid swimming, dusty outdoor activities, and contact sports for 2-4 weeks. Patients with confirmed AL or ATTR amyloidosis with cardiac involvement should consult cardiology before starting any high-intensity programme.
dailyManagement: Use preservative-free lubricating drops 4-6 times daily during dry, windy, or screen-heavy periods
Wear UV400 sunglasses and a hat every time you go outdoors, even on cloudy days
Note any change in lesion colour, size, vessel pattern, or bleeding and report it to your ophthalmologist promptly
Take prescribed postoperative drops on schedule and attend follow-up visits at 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, and then annually
Maintain medications for systemic amyloidosis exactly as prescribed and attend all haematology or cardiology follow-up appointments
prognosis: For primary localized amyloid pterygium, the prognosis after well-performed excision with conjunctival autograft is excellent: under 5% recurrence in most large series (Hirst 2008), with restoration of vision and a normal appearance in over 90% of patients. Astigmatism typically improves within 1-3 months, and the cosmetic result is stable by 6 months. When amyloid pterygium is the presenting sign of systemic AL amyloidosis, the long-term outlook depends on cardiac and renal involvement rather than the eye: untreated AL amyloid with cardiac involvement carries a median survival of 6-12 months, but modern bortezomib- and daratumumab-based regimens have raised 5-year survival above 60% in patients without advanced cardiac disease. ATTR amyloidosis prognosis depends on the variant and on access to tafamidis or RNA-silencing therapy, which can stabilize or improve cardiac and neurologic outcomes. Patients with AA amyloid do best when the underlying inflammatory disease is controlled. Lifelong UV protection, lubrication, and annual ophthalmology review are recommended for all subtypes.
recoveryTimeline: After pterygium excision with conjunctival autograft, the ocular surface re-epithelialises within 5-10 days. Most patients return to office work within a week and to outdoor work within 2-4 weeks. Topical steroid drops are tapered over 6-8 weeks. Astigmatism stabilises by 3 months. Cosmetic redness over the graft fades over 3-6 months. If mitomycin C was used, healing may be slower and follow-up runs to 6-12 months. Patients with confirmed systemic amyloid follow a separate haematology-led timeline that depends on the chemotherapy or amyloid-specific regimen used.
complications: Recurrence of the pterygium, particularly if bare sclera technique is used (30-90%) or in high-UV environments without postoperative protection
Persistent astigmatism, scarring, or symblepharon if the underlying Tenon's capsule is not adequately debulked at surgery
Scleral thinning or rare scleral melt from mitomycin C overuse
Postoperative steroid-induced rise in intraocular pressure or steroid-induced cataract in long courses
Bleeding from fragile amyloid vessels during minor trauma or in the early postoperative period
Missed systemic AL or ATTR amyloid leading to delayed cardiac, renal, or neurologic morbidity

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — EyeWiki on Pterygium
supportResources.description: Open-access clinician and patient resource covering pterygium pathology, surgery, and management updates.

supportResources.name: Amyloidosis Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization with education on AL, ATTR, and AA amyloidosis, plus a centre directory for specialist care.

supportResources.name: UK National Amyloidosis Centre
supportResources.description: Royal Free Hospital, London — leading European reference centre for systemic amyloid evaluation, including SAP scintigraphy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataract
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataract is a lens opacity inside the eye behind the pupil. Amyloid pterygium sits on the surface of the eye, visible on the white of the eye encroaching onto the cornea. Vision loss from cataract is gradual and central; from amyloid pterygium it is from astigmatism or growth onto the visual axis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Conjunctival or Eyelid Basal Cell Carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Basal cell carcinoma forms a pearly, sometimes ulcerated nodule with telangiectatic vessels on the eyelid skin or rarely the conjunctiva. Amyloid pterygium is a wedge-shaped waxy growth on the bulbar conjunctiva crossing onto the cornea, with characteristic Congo red staining on biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Conjunctival Melanoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Conjunctival melanoma is pigmented or mixed-pigment with feeder vessels and arises from primary acquired melanosis. Amyloid pterygium is yellow-pink and waxy; histology distinguishes the two definitively.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma is optic nerve damage with raised intraocular pressure and visual field loss; it is invisible on the front of the eye. Amyloid pterygium is a visible surface growth and does not raise intraocular pressure unless treated with steroids.

relatedConditions.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral idiopathic carpal tunnel in older adults is an early red flag for ATTR amyloidosis, which can also deposit amyloid in the conjunctiva.

relatedConditions.name: Heart Failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Amyloid cardiomyopathy from AL or ATTR amyloid presents with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction; an amyloid pterygium can be the first accessible biopsy site.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Long-standing uncontrolled rheumatoid arthritis can drive AA amyloid deposition, which may involve the conjunctiva in rare cases.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Sjogren-related dry eye worsens ocular surface inflammation, accelerates pterygium growth, and complicates postoperative healing.

relatedConditions.name: Myelodysplastic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Clonal plasma-cell disorders that produce monoclonal light chains can give rise to AL amyloid deposition in soft tissues, including the conjunctiva.

faqs.question: What is amyloid pterygium?
faqs.answer: Amyloid pterygium is a rare pterygium variant in which amyloid protein deposits build up inside the growth on the white of the eye. The lesion looks waxy and yellow-pink, bleeds more easily than a standard pterygium, and is confirmed by Congo red showing apple-green colour under polarized light.

faqs.question: How is amyloid pterygium different from a regular pterygium?
faqs.answer: A regular pterygium is a fleshy pink growth driven mainly by sun, wind, and dust. An amyloid pterygium has the same wedge shape but looks waxy and yellow-pink, has fragile vessels that bleed with minor trauma, and contains amyloid protein. Only Congo red biopsy tells them apart for certain.

faqs.question: Is amyloid pterygium dangerous?
faqs.answer: The eye lesion itself rarely threatens vision unless it grows onto the central cornea or causes large astigmatism. The main concern is that amyloid pterygium can signal systemic amyloidosis affecting the heart, kidneys, or nerves. Blood, urine, and heart tests after biopsy rule this out.

faqs.question: What causes amyloid pterygium?
faqs.answer: Two factors act together. First, chronic ultraviolet light, wind, and dust exposure drive ordinary pterygium formation. Second, amyloid protein deposits — either local to the eye or part of systemic light-chain or transthyretin amyloidosis. Tropical climates and outdoor work raise the risk of both.

faqs.question: How is amyloid pterygium diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by slit-lamp examination plus excisional biopsy. Congo red staining produces apple-green colour under polarized light when amyloid is present. The pathologist identifies the subtype; blood, urine, ECG, and echocardiogram follow if systemic disease is suspected.

faqs.question: Does amyloid pterygium need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is recommended when the lesion causes visually significant astigmatism, grows toward the centre of the cornea, bleeds repeatedly, or causes cosmetic distress. Small, stable, asymptomatic lesions can be observed every 6-12 months with lubricants and ultraviolet protection.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence rate after amyloid pterygium surgery?
faqs.answer: When excision is combined with a conjunctival autograft, recurrence is under 5% in most published series. Older bare-sclera techniques carry recurrence rates of 30-90% and should no longer be used. Adding intraoperative mitomycin C in high-risk eyes can lower recurrence further.

faqs.question: Will I need treatment for systemic amyloidosis?
faqs.answer: Only if the workup confirms it. Most cases are localized to the eye and need no systemic treatment. AL amyloid is managed by haematology with proteasome inhibitor regimens; ATTR is treated with tafamidis; AA amyloid requires control of the underlying inflammatory disease.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from amyloid pterygium surgery?
faqs.answer: The eye surface heals over 5-10 days. Most people return to office work within a week and to outdoor work within 2-4 weeks. Steroid drops are tapered over 6-8 weeks, and the graft redness settles over 3-6 months. Astigmatism stabilizes by 3 months in most patients.

faqs.question: Can amyloid pterygium come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence is more likely with bare sclera surgery, large or recurrent lesions, ongoing ultraviolet exposure, and untreated systemic amyloidosis. Conjunctival autograft, careful surgery, postoperative steroid drops, and lifelong sun protection give the best chance of staying recurrence-free.

faqs.question: Is amyloid pterygium hereditary?
faqs.answer: The pterygium itself is not inherited. However, transthyretin (ATTR) amyloidosis can be hereditary, and a family history of ATTR cardiomyopathy, peripheral neuropathy, or carpal tunnel syndrome in older relatives raises the chance that an amyloid pterygium reflects hereditary ATTR disease.

faqs.question: Can I prevent amyloid pterygium?
faqs.answer: Full prevention is not possible, but risk can be lowered. Wear UV400 sunglasses and a wide-brimmed hat outdoors, use preservative-free lubricant drops in dry or dusty settings, treat dry eye and blepharitis early, and control any chronic inflammation that could drive amyloid deposition.

faqs.question: Does amyloid pterygium affect vision?
faqs.answer: Often yes, in two ways. The growth can flatten the cornea and induce astigmatism that blurs vision. If the lesion crosses onto the central cornea, it can directly block the visual axis. Surgery removes both effects in most patients.

faqs.question: Why does amyloid pterygium bleed easily?
faqs.answer: Amyloid protein deposits around the small blood vessels in the lesion weaken their walls. Minor rubbing, dust, or wind can rupture these fragile vessels, producing the spontaneous redness and bleeding that distinguishes amyloid pterygium from the standard form.

faqs.question: Is amyloid pterygium more common in certain countries?
faqs.answer: Yes. It is reported most often in tropical and equatorial regions with high UV exposure — parts of South Asia, the Middle East, sub-Saharan Africa, and northern Australia. Standard pterygium reaches 10-12% in those regions and amyloid variants are more frequent than in temperate climates.

faqs.question: What does the surgery involve?
faqs.answer: The surgeon removes the pterygium under topical or subconjunctival anaesthesia, debulks the scar tissue, and stitches or glues a small healthy piece of conjunctiva from the upper part of the same eye onto the bare sclera. The procedure takes 30-45 minutes and is usually day-care.

faqs.question: Will the eye look red after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, for several weeks. The graft and surrounding conjunctiva are red and swollen early on, then settle over 3-6 months as healing completes. Most patients are satisfied with the cosmetic result by 6 months.

faqs.question: Can amyloid pterygium turn into cancer?
faqs.answer: Amyloid pterygium is a benign deposition disease, not a cancer. Rare cases coexist with ocular surface squamous neoplasia or primary acquired melanosis, which is why suspicious lesions go for full pathology with margin marking. Excision with autograft and biopsy resolves the question.

faqs.question: Do contact lenses make amyloid pterygium worse?
faqs.answer: Contact lenses do not cause pterygium, but in a patient with an existing lesion they can worsen surface dryness and irritation. Many patients switch to spectacles before surgery and during the first three months of postoperative healing.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist for amyloid pterygium?
faqs.answer: See a cornea-trained ophthalmologist for any yellow or waxy growth on the eye surface, a pterygium that bleeds easily, increasing astigmatism, or any pterygium within 3 millimetres of the central cornea. Early review allows planned surgery before the visual axis is involved.

simpleName: Amyloid Pterygium (waxy pterygium with amyloid protein)
metaDescription: Amyloid pterygium: a waxy, bleeding-prone pterygium variant. Symptoms, Congo red diagnosis, surgery, and systemic amyloid workup reviewed by our board.
